title: Math Annual Update for Program Review
text:
College of the Siskiyous
Annual Program Update
Mathematics Department
Annual progress/changes/events in the program:
The 2017- 2018 academic year was the first year the Mathematics department no longer
offered Elementary Algebra. This was the most significant change for the department. There
were no changes in other classes offered, and there were no events.
Progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program:
The department has been assessing SLOs. If multiple sections of a course are being
offered in a given semester, the instructors agree which SLO will be assessed that semester so all
sections are considering the same SLO. SLOs assessed in single course offerings are at the
discretion of the instructor. All SLOs correspond to the department’s PLO. Since there has been
no request for the department’s SLO report, no formal report has been compiled. However, each
instructor has kept his or her SLO data, so a formal report could be complied if needed (with the
possible exception of adjunct instructors who no longer work for the district).
Support or obstacles encountered by the program:
The fact that the department no longer offers Elementary Algebra has been an obstacle
since some students, who enroll in Intermediate Algebra, have gaps in their Algebra skills—or
perhaps have no algebra skills—that the Elementary Algebra classes used to fill. As support,
embedded tutors have been placed in all Elementary Statistics classes as well as in some
Intermediate Algebra classes to provide “just in time remediation” to students who are deficient
in necessary skills.
Budgetary needs or implications:
With the introduction of AB705, the Mathematics department is left in a quandary as to
the proper way to accommodate students in light of this new legislation. At this point in time,
there is no clear solution which will guarantee student success once this new legislation is in
place. It also appears that the grant which funded the salaries for some of the embedded tutors is
coming to an end. Therefore, the department is requesting budget funds which will help pay the
salaries for embedded tutors in Elementary Statistics classes. The department is also requesting
budget funds for travel expenses and registration fees, so instructors may attend conferences such
as CMC^3 and AMATYC. Conferences like these would help the instructors to become better
informed about possible solutions to the issues surrounding AB705, which would help improve
student success. The department would also like to purchase the Maple program for use in the
STEM classes being offered. In addition, the department has also discussed with administration
the possibility of creating an online version of the Elementary Statistics class. Thus, the
department is also requesting funds to be used as stipends for those who work on creating this
online class. Please see accompanying CQIPs for proposed budget expenses.
